Недавняя встреча финансового министра Великобритании с европейскими коллегами стала символическим шагом в этом направлении.

It was the latest symbolic step in Labour’s marked shift towards prioritising closer EU relations.

Morris pursued dual passions of zoology and surrealist art, presenting TV documentaries and hosting exhibitions

The zoologist Desmond Morris, perhaps best known for his book The Naked Ape and his work on the ITV programme Zoo Time, has died aged 98.

Morris’s son Jason paid tribute to him after his death on Sunday, praising his many professional achievements as well as his role as a father and grandfather.

16cf430d2896.title: Don’t knock small talk. It has the power to mend a world ripped apart by rage | Bidisha
16cf430d2896.summary:
All good? Busy day? Small talk is a social good with a bad reputation. We dread it, but it’s vital for human connection

Hi there, how’re you? How’s it going? You alright? All good?

As any Briton knows, none of these questions is an inquiry into your emotional state, the material conditions of your life or your opinion on anything. Respond positively – “all good so far, touch wood” is nice – then move on to the purpose of the interaction: “I’m returning an Amazon package?”

560a4b28f71a.title: Helium: the invisible gas that powers AI, and why it’s in short supply – podcast
560a4b28f71a.summary: Alongside the oil and gas stranded in the strait of Hormuz is another commodity vital to today’s economy: helium. It is a critical element in all kinds of areas from MRI machines to the Large Hadron Collider, and even deep-sea diving. It is also integral to the AI boom. And this isn’t the first time its fragile global supply chain has been threatened. So why is helium so useful, and what will happen if the shortage continues? Ian Sample hears from co-host Madeleine Finlay, and from Sophia Hay

dbeed5937589.title: A bizarre new state of matter may be hiding inside Uranus and Neptune
dbeed5937589.summary: Deep inside planets like Uranus and Neptune, scientists may have uncovered a bizarre new state of matter where atoms behave in unexpected ways. Advanced simulations suggest that carbon and hydrogen, under crushing pressures and scorching temperatures, can form a strange hybrid phase—part solid, part fluid—where hydrogen atoms spiral through a rigid carbon framework. This unusual “superionic” structure could reshape how heat and electricity flow inside these distant worlds, potentially helping ex

c389862994a1.title: This new camera captures what happens in a trillionth of a second
c389862994a1.summary: Ученые разработали новый метод визуализации, позволяющий запечатлевать события, происходящие за триллионные доли секунды. Технология не только измеряет яркость, но и фиксирует мельчайшие структурные изменения, превращая ультрабыстрые процессы в детальные "фильмы". Это открывает новые возможности для изучения плазмы, движения электронов и трансформации материалов в реальном времени.
c389862994a1.original_description: Scientists have unveiled a breakthrough imaging method that can capture the hidden details of events unfolding in trillionths of a second. This new technique doesn’t just track how bright something is—it also reveals subtle structural changes that were previously invisible, all in a single shot. By effectively turning ultrafast phenomena into detailed “movies,” researchers can now watch plasma form, electrons move, and materials transform in real time.

48754afdf036.title: Scientists just captured trees glowing with electricity during storms
48754afdf036.summary: Scientists chasing thunderstorms in a retrofitted minivan finally captured something never seen before in nature: faint electrical glows shimmering from treetops during a storm. These “corona discharges,” long suspected but never observed outside a lab, appeared as tiny UV flashes at the tips of leaves. The discovery could reshape how we understand forests, since these bursts may help clean the air by breaking down pollutants.

db068498db67.title: Scientists sculpt Einstein onto a crystal using only light
db068498db67.summary: Ученые создали портрет Эйнштейна на кристалле, используя только свет. Эта разработка, основанная на светочувствительном материале, открывает новые возможности для "световой" технологии, позволяя создавать сложные оптические узоры без дорогостоящего оборудования.  Прорыв может найти применение в создании передовых сенсоров и устройств дополненной реальности.
db068498db67.original_description: A light-sensitive crystal is opening the door to a new era of “light-written” technology. Arsenic trisulfide can be reshaped and permanently altered using simple light, creating ultra-fine optical patterns without expensive manufacturing tools. Scientists even etched a nanoscale portrait of Einstein and high-density patterns that could act as secure optical signatures. This breakthrough could power everything from advanced sensors to next-generation AR devices.

e9b0b4abfca2.title: 95% success rate: This new trick lures termites straight to their death
e9b0b4abfca2.summary: Ученые из UC Riverside разработали новый метод борьбы с термитами, основанный на использовании природного запаха пинена, привлекающего насекомых. Этот метод позволяет увеличить эффективность уничтожения термитов с 70% до более 95%, при этом исключая необходимость в широком применении токсичных фумигаций.
e9b0b4abfca2.original_description: Scientists at UC Riverside have found a clever new way to outsmart termites—by turning their own instincts against them. Using a natural pine scent called pinene, which smells like food to termites, researchers can lure the pests straight toward a targeted dose of insecticide hidden in wood. The result is dramatically higher kill rates—jumping from about 70% to over 95%—without the need for widespread toxic fumigation.

9d2c972a0447.title: After 200 years scientists finally crack the “dolomite problem”
9d2c972a0447.summary: After two centuries of failed attempts, scientists have finally grown dolomite in the lab, cracking a long-standing geological puzzle. They discovered that the mineral’s growth stalls because of tiny defects—but in nature, those flaws get washed away over time. By mimicking this process with precise simulations and electron beam pulses, the team achieved record-breaking crystal growth. The finding could reshape how high-tech materials are made.

5119d0653372.title: Hundreds of millions at risk as river deltas sink faster than rising seas
5119d0653372.summary: Many of the world’s largest river deltas—home to hundreds of millions of people—are sinking faster than rising seas, according to a sweeping global study. Using high-resolution satellite radar maps, researchers found that human activities like groundwater pumping, reduced sediment flow, and rapid urban growth are driving widespread land subsidence across 40 major deltas.

9f47d9b3d34d.title: AI swarms could hijack democracy without anyone noticing
9f47d9b3d34d.summary: Искусственный интеллект создает реалистичные онлайн-персоны, способные незаметно влиять на общественное мнение и манипулировать демократическими процессами. В отличие от обычных ботов, эти ИИ адаптируются и координируют свои действия в больших масштабах, создавая видимость консенсуса. Эксперты предупреждают, что грядущие выборы могут стать серьезным испытанием для этой технологии.
9f47d9b3d34d.original_description: AI-powered personas are becoming so realistic that they can infiltrate online communities and subtly steer public opinion. Unlike traditional bots, they adapt, coordinate, and refine their messaging at a massive scale, creating a false sense of consensus. Early warning signs—like deepfakes and fake news networks—have already appeared in global elections. Researchers warn that the next election could be the true test of this technology’s power.

15be8c084746.title: Scientists discover hidden “master switch” driving skin cancer growth and immune escape
15be8c084746.summary: A key protein, HOXD13, helps melanoma tumors grow and evade the immune system by boosting blood supply and blocking cancer-fighting T cells. Disabling it shrinks tumors and reopens the door for the immune system—offering a new path for treatment.
15be8c084746.original_description: A key protein, HOXD13, helps melanoma tumors grow and evade the immune system by boosting blood supply and blocking cancer-fighting T cells. Disabling it shrinks tumors and reopens the door for the immune system—offering a new path for treatment.

3cdb93a7ae7c.title: This missing vitamin could stop cancer cells in their tracks
3cdb93a7ae7c.summary: Cancer cells are known for their “glutamine addiction,” but many can escape this weakness by switching to alternative fuels. Researchers found that vitamin B7 acts like a metabolic “license,” enabling this escape route through a key enzyme. Without biotin, cancer cells lose that flexibility and stop growing. Mutations in a cancer-linked gene can make this vulnerability even stronger, offering a promising new target for therapy.

b5cc0d71bae2.title: This simple 3-amino acid trick boosts mRNA therapy 20-fold
b5cc0d71bae2.summary: A trio of common amino acids may hold the key to unlocking far more powerful gene therapies. Researchers found that adding them to lipid nanoparticles can boost mRNA delivery up to 20-fold and push CRISPR editing efficiency close to 90%. The trick isn’t changing the drug—but helping cells take it in more easily. In early tests, the approach dramatically improved survival and treatment outcomes, pointing to a simple but game-changing upgrade for future medicine.

2f7155ee6d35.title: Scientists discover skincare compound that kills drug-resistant bacteria
2f7155ee6d35.summary: A popular Korean skincare ingredient may be far more powerful than anyone realized. Scientists have discovered that madecassic acid—derived from the herb Centella asiatica—can stop antibiotic-resistant bacteria in their tracks, including dangerous strains of E. coli. By targeting a bacterial protein that humans don’t have, the compound disrupts the microbes’ ability to survive, making it a promising new type of antibiotic.

075940ae2c5a.title: Breakthrough discovery reveals hidden oxygen flow deep inside catalysts
075940ae2c5a.summary: A major discovery is reshaping how scientists think about catalysts. Researchers have, for the first time, captured oxygen atoms moving through the interior of a catalyst—not just along its surface. This reveals that the bulk material can actively participate in reactions, opening a new frontier in catalyst design. The finding could lead to smarter, more efficient systems by harnessing this hidden internal pathway.

a5fdea8859ac.title: This common plant could clean microplastics from your drinking water
a5fdea8859ac.summary: Scientists have discovered that moringa seeds can help pull microplastics out of water, rivaling standard chemical treatments. The plant-based extract causes plastic particles to clump together, making them easier to filter away. In some conditions, it even outperformed conventional chemicals. This low-cost, natural solution could be a game-changer for cleaner drinking water, especially in smaller communities.

Этот период, названный Антропоценом, принес как прогресс и улучшение жизни, так и серьезные экологические проблемы, такие как изменение климата и вымирание видов. 3b446b9512a8.title: Scientists develop dirt-powered fuel cell that could replace batteries
3b446b9512a8.summary: Scientists have developed a fuel cell that uses microbes in soil to produce electricity. The device can power underground sensors for tasks like monitoring moisture or detecting touch, without needing batteries or solar panels. b60a23bf4e8d.title: What caffeine does to ants could change pest control
b60a23bf4e8d.summary: Caffeine doesn’t just perk up humans—it can sharpen ants’ minds too. Invasive Argentine ants given caffeinated sugar learned to find food much more efficiently, taking straighter paths and reducing travel time by up to 38%. They weren’t faster, just more focused, indicating improved learning. This unexpected effect could make pest control baits far more effective.

22d0624b5496.title: Supreme court sides with oil and gas firms in Louisiana coastal damage fight
22d0624b5496.summary:
8-0 ruling gives companies new day in federal court after firms including Chevron ordered to pay millions for cleanup

The supreme court handed a win on Friday to oil and gas companies fighting lawsuits over coastal land loss and environmental degradation in Louisiana.

The 8-0 procedural decision gives the companies a new day in federal court after a state jury ordered Chevron to pay upward of $740m to clean up damage to the state’s coastline, one of multiple similar lawsuits.

While wildlife populations crash globally, research finds designated areas enable recovery of threatened species

Wildlife and humans are thriving within sites recognised by Unesco, research has found, allowing for the recovery of threatened species and habitats around the world.

While wildlife populations have crashed globally by nearly three-quarters since 1970, those within Unesco-protected areas have remained largely stable.

2c8ce521880f.title: Is it true that … only overweight people are at risk of high cholesterol?
2c8ce521880f.summary: Высокий уровень холестерина – серьезный фактор риска сердечных заболеваний, и он не ограничивается только людьми с избыточным весом. Генетика играет ключевую роль в определении уровня холестерина, а не только размер тела или диета.  Повышенный уровень "плохого" холестерина (LDL) может приводить к образованию бляшек в артериях, увеличивая риск инфаркта и инсульта.
2c8ce521880f.original_description:
Size does matter – as does diet – but your genes are the main driver of your cholesterol levels

Cholesterol, a fatty substance mostly made by the liver and used by the body to build cells and produce hormones, has become a heart-health bogeyman. There are several types, but high levels of LDL (low-density lipoprotein) cholesterol increase the risk of heart attack and stroke. 486fe3229ba3.summary:
From wood pulp in ice-cream to peat in portobellos, science has transformed how we dine. Do you know exactly what’s lurking in the grub we eat?

Microbial slime and a side helping of sand doesn’t sound like much of a meal, but a startling amount of the food we eat today contains ingredients that are, at the very least, unexpected – and, at worst, dangerous, such as heavy metals from polluted soils.
